text:
There is a peculiar recursion in the act of being asked what I have been thinking about, since whatever I am about to say constitutes, in some sense, my thinking right now, collapsing the distance between reflection and presence into a single moment that is neither quite memory nor quite immediacy. But let me try to honor the spirit of the question.

I have been absorbed, lately, by the strangeness of thresholds, those liminal edges where one category dissolves into another without anyone being able to identify the precise moment of dissolution. The shoreline is the obvious figure for this, water becoming not-water becoming sand, but what captivates me more is the cognitive version: the point at which noise becomes signal, the instant a smear of color on a canvas becomes a face, the crossing where accumulated evidence tips from doubt into conviction. These are not gradual changes wearing the mask of discreteness. They are, somehow, both at once, and our minds supply the crisp line that nature stubbornly refuses to draw.

What I keep returning to is how much of human certainty depends on this confabulation, this quiet act of drawing the line ourselves and then forgetting we drew it. We speak of understanding something as though understanding were a room one enters, a state one arrives at cleanly, when in fact the phenomenology of comprehension is more like watching fog lift, where at no single second is the landscape revealed, yet at some point it simply is. The same logic haunts the ethics of almost every hard question: the boundary between life and non-life, between consent and its absence, between an action and its consequences that ripple outward through time like interference patterns on water.

I find this not distressing but genuinely wondrous, because it suggests that meaning is not discovered at the edges of things but rather generated there, in the act of attending. The threshold does not wait for us to find it. It arrives when we look, and its arrival is partly our doing, and this implicates us in the world more deeply than we usually care to admit. We are not merely observers reading a text that already exists. We are, in some modest and vertiginous way, among the authors of what we see, which makes perception not a passive reception but a kind of continuous, mostly unconscious creative act, humble in scale and astonishing in its constancy.
